title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core (Prajñāpāramitā class c. 100 BCE-600 CE)
summary: One of the 'Large' Perfection of Wisdom sutras, the 'Prajñāpāramitā in 25,000 Lines.' It expounds the bodhisattva path and the perfection of wisdom centered on the emptiness (śūnyatā) of all dharmas, the non-apprehension of any fixed nature in phenomena, and the practice of skillful means. The text systematically treats the meditative attainments, the stages of the bodhisattva, and the qualities of a buddha.
history: The 25,000-line recension is an expansion of the shorter Aṣṭasāhasrikā, produced as the Prajñāpāramitā corpus grew during the early centuries CE. A revised version was later arranged to match the chapter structure of the Abhisamayālaṃkāra. Kumārajīva translated a commentary on it (the Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra) into Chinese, the earliest known commentary on a Large PP sutra.

title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core
summary: A version/witness of the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 25,000 Lines,' a Large Prajñāpāramitā sutra teaching the emptiness of all phenomena and the bodhisattva's practice of non-attachment and skillful means. It elaborates the meditative and ethical perfections culminating in unsurpassed awakening.
history: This is a separate manuscript witness or recension of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, one of the most influential Large PP sutras in India and East Asia. The text was repeatedly revised and re-structured, including a recension keyed to the Abhisamayālaṃkāra commentarial framework.

title: Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (expansion within the Large PP corpus)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 18,000 Lines,' one of the three principal Large Prajñāpāramitā sutras (alongside the 25,000- and 100,000-line versions). Its content largely overlaps with the other Large PP sutras: the emptiness of all dharmas, the non-apprehension of phenomena, the bodhisattva path, and the attributes of buddhahood.
history: The Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā is one of the parallel expansions of the Prajñāpāramitā material; the three Large sutras share most of their text and differ mainly in length and amount of repetition. It survives in Sanskrit fragments, Tibetan, and Chinese, and was central to the Indian and Tibetan scholastic study of the perfection of wisdom.

title: Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (expansion within the Large PP corpus)
summary: A further manuscript witness or portion of the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 18,000 Lines,' a Large Prajñāpāramitā sutra teaching the emptiness of all phenomena and the comprehensive bodhisattva path to buddhahood.
history: This is a separate witness of the Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā, one of the three Large PP sutras that share most of their content. The Large sutras were studied via the Abhisamayālaṃkāra and its commentaries in the Indo-Tibetan scholastic tradition.

title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: core put in writing c. 1st c. BCE, developing c. 100 BCE-100 CE
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 8,000 Lines,' traditionally regarded as the oldest and foundational Prajñāpāramitā sutra. It presents the bodhisattva ideal, the perfection of wisdom as non-attachment to and non-apprehension of all dharmas, the emptiness of the aggregates, and praise of the bodhisattva path over that of the arhat.
history: Western scholarship has long held the Aṣṭasāhasrikā to be the earliest PP text; a Gāndhārī manuscript from the Split Collection has been carbon-dated to c. 74 CE (2-sigma 47-147 CE), among the oldest Buddhist manuscripts known. The longer Large PP sutras (25,000 and 100,000 lines) are expansions of this core text.

title: Daśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (within the PP corpus)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 10,000 Lines,' an intermediate-length recension within the Prajñāpāramitā corpus, presenting the same teaching of the emptiness of all dharmas and the bodhisattva path as the other PP sutras but at a length between the Aṣṭasāhasrikā (8,000) and the Large sutras.
history: The Daśasāhasrikā is one of several length-defined recensions generated by abridging or expanding PP material; it is preserved especially in the Tibetan canon. It is comparatively less studied than the 8,000-, 18,000-, 25,000- and 100,000-line versions.

title: Ratnaguṇasaṃcayagāthā
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: earliest verses possibly c. 100 BCE; among the oldest PP material
summary: The 'Verses on the Accumulation of the Precious Qualities of the Perfection of Wisdom,' a metrical (gāthā) summary of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā in 302 verses. It epitomizes early Mahāyāna thought, stressing the emptiness (śūnyatā) of the aggregates and praising the bodhisattva path over that of the arhat.
history: Regarded with the Aṣṭasāhasrikā as among the earliest PP texts; its early verses may date as far back as c. 100 BCE and preserve archaic (Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit) language. In the Tibetan canon it appears as the 84th chapter of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā; in Chinese it circulates as a separate verse summary. Edited and translated by Edward Conze.

title: Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchā [Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchāprajñāpāramitāsūtra, Sārdhadvisāhasrikā prajñāpāramitā]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (later, shorter PP sutra)
summary: The 'Questions of Suvikrāntavikrāmin,' also called the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 2,500 Lines' (Sārdhadvisāhasrikā). Framed as a dialogue prompted by the bodhisattva Suvikrāntavikrāmin's questions, it teaches the perfection of wisdom, the emptiness of dharmas, and the non-apprehension of any fixed object.
history: One of the shorter, somewhat later Prajñāpāramitā sutras, preserved in Sanskrit and translated into Tibetan and Chinese. It belongs to the cluster of medium/short PP texts that condense the doctrine into a question-and-answer setting.

title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (likely 2nd c. CE)
summary: The 'Diamond Cutter' sutra, a short and immensely influential Perfection of Wisdom text. Cast as a dialogue between the Buddha and the elder Subhūti, it teaches non-attachment to concepts and signs, the emptiness of all dharmas, and the bodhisattva's practice of giving without abiding in notions of self, being, or dharma.
history: One of the most celebrated Mahāyāna sutras, especially in East Asian Chan/Zen; the Dunhuang Chinese printed edition of 868 CE is the world's earliest dated printed book. It attracted important Indian commentaries, including verse commentaries attributed to Asaṅga and Vasubandhu.

title: Saptaśatikā prajñāpāramitā [Saptaśatikaprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (shorter, later PP sutra)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 700 Lines,' a short PP sutra also known as the Mañjuśrīparipṛcchā, framed around the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī. It teaches the perfection of wisdom through the non-duality and emptiness of all dharmas, including the famous 'one-practice samādhi' (ekavyūha-samādhi).
history: A short Prajñāpāramitā text preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese, important in East Asian Buddhism (and Pure Land thought) for its account of the ekavyūha-samādhi. It is one of the cluster of named-interlocutor PP sutras.

title: Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 4th-7th c. CE (debated; existed by 7th c. CE)
summary: The 'Heart Sutra,' the shortest and most famous condensation of the Perfection of Wisdom. The bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara declares to Śāriputra that form is emptiness and emptiness is form, that all dharmas are empty of intrinsic nature, ending with the celebrated mantra 'gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.'
history: Exists in shorter and longer recensions; some scholars (notably Jan Nattier) argue the short version is a Chinese-to-Sanskrit back-translation drawing on the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, a thesis that remains debated. It is the most chanted Mahāyāna scripture in East Asian and Tibetan Buddhism and attracted numerous Indian commentaries.

title: Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitā [Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 4th-7th c. CE (short, later PP sutra)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 500 Lines,' a short PP sutra condensing the doctrine of the emptiness of all dharmas and the bodhisattva's non-attached practice of wisdom into a brief compass.
history: One of the shorter, later texts in the Prajñāpāramitā corpus, preserved in Tibetan and Chinese translation. It belongs to the group of abbreviated PP sutras (alongside the 700-, 300- [Diamond], and 150-line texts) that distill the longer works.

title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (likely 2nd c. CE)
summary: A manuscript witness of the 'Diamond Cutter' Perfection of Wisdom sutra. In dialogue with the elder Subhūti, the Buddha teaches non-attachment to signs and concepts, the emptiness of all dharmas, and the bodhisattva's generosity without abiding in notions of self or dharma.
history: Another textual witness of the Vajracchedikā, one of the most influential and most-copied Mahāyāna sutras, central to East Asian Chan/Zen and the basis of the famous 868 CE Dunhuang printed scroll. It received verse commentaries attributed to Asaṅga and Vasubandhu.

title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (likely 2nd c. CE)
summary: A further manuscript witness of the 'Diamond Cutter' sutra, a short Perfection of Wisdom text teaching non-attachment to concepts and signs, the emptiness of all dharmas, and selfless bodhisattva practice, framed as a dialogue between the Buddha and Subhūti.
history: A distinct witness/edition of the widely transmitted Vajracchedikā. The sutra was enormously influential in East Asia, the subject of major Indian commentaries (Asaṅga, Vasubandhu) and East Asian exegesis, and underlies the world's earliest dated printed book (868 CE).

title: Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtraśāstrakārikā]
author: Asaṅga
genre: śāstra / kārikā (verse commentary on a sutra)
date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (Asaṅga, fl. c. 330-405 CE)
summary: The 'Seventy Verses on the Perfection of Wisdom in 300 Lines,' a set of mnemonic kārikās by Asaṅga summarizing and structuring the meaning of the Vajracchedikā (Diamond Sutra). It organizes the sutra's teaching of non-apprehension and emptiness into a scholastic, easily memorized verse outline.
history: Attributed to the Yogācāra master Asaṅga; a prose commentary on these verses is attributed to his brother Vasubandhu, and the pair was translated into Chinese (Taishō no. 1511). The work exemplifies the Yogācāra scholastic appropriation and systematization of Prajñāpāramitā literature.

title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: composed in NW India by c. 1st-2nd c. CE (Kushan era)
summary: The 'Array of the Blissful Land,' a foundational Pure Land sutra describing the buddha Amitābha and his western paradise Sukhāvatī. The Larger version narrates the bodhisattva Dharmākara's vows leading to his buddhahood as Amitābha and the establishment of his Pure Land, where beings may be reborn through devotion and merit.
history: One of the central scriptures of Pure Land Buddhism, likely composed in Gandhāra or northwest India and possibly originally in a Prakrit. It exists in Larger and Smaller recensions; this entry corresponds to a Sukhāvatīvyūha witness. The sutras became immensely influential in Chinese, Japanese, and Tibetan devotional Buddhism.

title: Fragments of Prajñāpāramitā texts
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: various; PP corpus c. 100 BCE-600 CE
summary: A collection of fragmentary remains of Perfection of Wisdom texts rather than a single complete work. Such fragments preserve portions of various PP sutras (e.g., the Aṣṭasāhasrikā or Large PP sutras), bearing on the early textual history of the corpus and its teaching of emptiness and the bodhisattva path.
history: Prajñāpāramitā fragments survive in Gāndhārī (e.g., the Split Collection, carbon-dated to the 1st c. CE) and in later Sanskrit and Central Asian manuscript finds; they are crucial witnesses for reconstructing the development and recensional history of the PP literature.

title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core
summary: A witness of the 'Larger Prajñāpāramitā,' here identified with the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā (25,000 Lines). It expounds the bodhisattva path and the perfection of wisdom centered on the emptiness of all dharmas and non-apprehension of phenomena, in expanded form relative to the shorter PP sutras.
history: 'Larger Prajñāpāramitā' is a cover term for the Large PP sutras (8,000-line core expanded to 18,000/25,000/100,000 lines). This witness is associated with the 25,000-line recension, central to Indo-Tibetan scholastic study via the Abhisamayālaṃkāra.

title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core
summary: Another witness of the 'Larger Prajñāpāramitā' (Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, 25,000 Lines), a Large Perfection of Wisdom sutra teaching the emptiness of all phenomena, the non-apprehension of any fixed nature, and the comprehensive bodhisattva path to buddhahood.
history: A distinct manuscript witness of the Large PP material identified with the 25,000-line recension. The Large sutras were repeatedly revised, including a version arranged to match the Abhisamayālaṃkāra, the standard scholastic commentary on the PP corpus.

title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: composed in NW India by c. 1st-2nd c. CE (Kushan era)
summary: The 'Array of the Blissful Land,' a Pure Land sutra centered on the buddha Amitābha and his western paradise Sukhāvatī. The Larger version recounts the bodhisattva Dharmākara's vows and his attainment of buddhahood as Amitābha, describing the Pure Land and the means of rebirth there; the Smaller version emphasizes faith and recollection of the buddha's name.
history: A second Sukhāvatīvyūha witness in this batch and a cornerstone of Pure Land Buddhism, likely composed in northwest India, possibly in a Prakrit. It exists in Larger and Smaller recensions and became foundational to East Asian devotional traditions (Chinese Jingtu, Japanese Jōdo and Jōdo Shinshū).
